risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Families completing pre-intervention assessments were randomly assigned to MSFP (n = 154), SFP 10-14 (n = 160), or home study (n = 118)." Comment: The paper explicitly states that families were randomly assigned, and the results section notes randomization occurred within cohort/site as the blocking unit. Although the exact random generation mechanism is not described, the combination of random assignment and blocking context is sufficient to judge this as a proper random sequence for this domain under permissive criteria.

Summary: The study states that families completing pre-intervention assessments were 'randomly assigned' to the three conditions, but it gives no description of how the assignment was concealed from the recruiters/enrolling staff. There is no explicit mention of central allocation, sealed opaque sequential envelopes, identical containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment. Comment: Random assignment alone is insufficient to judge allocation concealment, so this domain remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The intervention was behavioral and delivered in groups, with one condition receiving added mindfulness activities and the other receiving standard family skills training, so participants and facilitators would have been aware of assignment. The paper does not describe any blinding of participants or personnel. Because the main outcomes included self-reported parenting, mindfulness, relationship quality, and behavior management, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence reporting and delivery.

Summary: Outcome assessment was by mothers’ self-report, fathers’ self-report, and youths’ report of parents’ functioning, with no statement that these respondents were blinded to allocation. The paper states: "Mothers and fathers reported on their own functioning, and/or youths reported on their parents’ functioning" and the outcomes include subjective/self-reported constructs such as interpersonal mindfulness in parenting, parent-youth relationship quality, youth behavior management, and parent well-being. Because the assessors were the participants themselves (and youth) and knowledge of group assignment could influence these subjective ratings, detection bias is likely.

Quote: "To reduce any possible bias due to missing data, all results reflect the combined estimates from 50 multiple imputation datasets" Comment: The paper explicitly addresses missing data with multiple imputation, and the trial appears to have follow-up assessments at post-intervention and one year with a randomized sample of 432 families. Although the exact per-arm attrition numbers are not provided in the excerpt, the reported use of multiple imputation and the lack of any signal that dropout was imbalanced or related to outcomes make attrition bias unlikely.
